figures.caption: Table 3: Genomic Benchmarks results. Top-1 accuracy (↑) is reported across 5-fold cross-validation. Best values per task are bolded, second-best values are underlined, and ± indicates the standard deviation across 5 random seeds .
Figure 2: Comparison of Fourier Position Embedding (FoPE) and Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) in pretraining on the hg38 dataset using Wisteria-100M with different length.
Figure 3: UMAP visualization of embeddings for short sequence regions with and without the gated convolution branch.

Table 9: N-block Allocation results on Nucleotide Transformer tasks. N denotes the number of shallow GCMB blocks. The N=5 configuration corresponds to the final Wisteria architecture used in Figure 1. Best values are highlighted in bold, and second-best values are underlined.

Table 4: Nucleotide Transformer benchmark results. MCC (↑) is reported for histone mark and enhancer prediction tasks, F1-score (↑) for promoter and splice-site identification tasks, and accuracy (↑) for “Splice All.” Best values per task are bolded, second-best values are underlined, and ± indicates the variation across ten random seeds.
Table 1: Pretraining hyperparameters. Values used during pretraining are reported.

Figure 4: Performance metrics at different sequence lengths. The line plots show the variation in throughput (K tokens/s) and peak memory (MB) with respect to sequence length for architectures with and without the attention layer.
Figure 1: The framework of the proposed Wisteria. The architecture is consist of Gated Convolution– BiMamba (GCMB) modules, gated MLP modules, and a final Fourier based attention layer.
Table 8: Ablation study results on Nucleotide Transformer tasks. Best values are highlighted in bold, and second-best values are underlined.
